data.title: Camping World Holdings: Not A Bottom, Could Get Cheaper
data.description:
Camping World Holdings (NYSE:CWH) is the largest RV dealership in the US. It became publicly traded in 2016 as a result of the merger between Camping World and Good Sams. Below is the share price performance since IPO:

Their two business segments are RV & Outdoor Retail, and Good Sam Services and Plans, with the former making up over 90% of revenue. Lets compare some return metrics with the closest peers:

Capital Allocation

Now let see how capital has been allocated for the past decade:

They have been employing a rollup strategy in this fragmented industry for a while now. A roll up strategy isn’t inherently bad or good. I’m also not against using debt as the primary means of funding acquisitions, but I prefer to see a more defined ‘growth phase’ where deleveraging takes priority, followed by returning capital to shareholders once growth, more importantly the returns on incremental growth, has essentially peaked. Instead CWH does pay a dividend and occasionally buys back shares, but they also continually use long term debt to fund acquisitions in addition to diluting equity which they did last year.

Overall I wouldn’t give a high grade for capital allocation, and the use of debt ties into the risk, which I will cover next.

Risk

The cyclicality is an obvious trait and therefore a risk. A booming economy is better for sales and service of RVs than a struggling economy. This isn’t where I see inherent risk necessarily.

For me, the most unappealing aspect of the RV dealership model is the necessity of floor plan financing. To sum it up briefly, most vehicle dealerships use a special line of credit known as ‘floor plan financing’ wherein the vehicle is used as collateral, and interest is charged on each vehicle for as long as it sits unsold on the lot. This arrangement can’t be avoided, but inventory turns are obviously higher for a traditional car dealership than an RV dealership.

The majority of floor plan financing falls under shorter term debt. So the $2.3 billion of long term debt is mostly for funding acquisitions. This is of course a business that enjoys tailwinds in a low interest rate environment. It can borrow more cheaply to fund acquisitions, and customers are more likely to finance an RV with lower rates.

Overall I think CWH chases growth for growth’s sake, and sees its size as its moat. And its true that no one is likely to copy their strategy and one day take significant market share. I don’t consider this a true moat. This model works for a similar public company, Lithia Motors (LAD), but they simply acquire traditional car dealerships. Having cars instead of RVs as inventory inherently means higher inventory turns, so its obviously not fair to make a direct comparison, but it shows how tough the business model is.

I personally think the academic way of looking at value creation being the mathematical outcome of ROIC minus WACC is too rigid, it doesn’t apply to many types of companies. In this case however, I think it does. It could certainly be worse, but right now there is a clear difference between the cost of capital and the returns on capital. Consolidating increases the top line, but the overall quality is not improving as the company grows.

Valuation

I’d like to start by saying that I was a big fan of CEO Marcus Lemonis’s show “The Profit” back when it was on air on CNBC. I’ll do my best to not be biased in my valuation, this article is analyzing the company, not the man.

Lets look first at multiples compared to the current to peers:

Company

EV/Sales

EV/EBITDA

EV/FCF

P/B

Div Yield

CWH

0.4

8.4

-149.3

The stock looks cheap on a multiples basis, but as always, this doesn't tell the full story. It shows how the market is pricing the stock, but lets now go to the DCF model:

The TTM EPS for CWH are currently negative so the EPS is normalized. I wouldn’t say that I’m being overly conservative, but EPS growth will always be capped to an extent with so much long term debt being used to fund growth.

We also know that we can’t count on share count being reduced, its actually increased over the past few years. Organic growth is very stagnant in this economy. I’m not saying there is a secular downward trend, RV’s are actually popular with younger generations. My point is simply that the only growth is from acquisitions, and I think there is already too much debt being used to fund growth.

This isn’t the kind of qualitative stock I look for, but there have certainly been opportunities for shorter term value trades in the past. At a certain price, the situation could become interesting, but that price isn’t here yet. As cheap at this stock may appear now, I’m not convinced that we are at a cyclical bottom. There is still a significant amount of economic uncertainty in regards to the next few years, especially in the context of discretionary goods that the middle class can or can’t afford. I’m giving the stock a “hold” rating today, and it won’t be added to any qualitative watchlist of mine

Conclusion

While I was a big fan of the CEO’s TV show back when it first aired, I’m not a fan of this business model overall. Floor plan financing, plus a highly discretionary product presents big challenges inherently. Add in mediocre capital allocation and growth for growth’s sake, and you have a recipe for avoidance for the more qualitative investor.

At the end of the day there isn’t enough value to be had for being the biggest in this space, and continually rolling up. Size can’t be your only moat, and there is definitely no improvement in quality that comes with scaling. Not only is size alone not a real moat, but its a burden when the company becomes overloaded with debt. The company is a “hold” for me, because while it may appear cheap at first glance, I’m not confident this is a bottom, or that the price can’t go lower still.

data.title: WisdomTree: Recent Initiatives Strengthen The Bull Case
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, WisdomTree, Inc. (NYSE:WT) has good long-term growth prospects, as the company is focused on passive investing and diversifying even further its product offering, namely through new areas such as digital assets and more recently private assets.

Despite this background, its business is still largely exposed to market sentiment and flows on the capital markets, which led to some share price weakness a few months ago. When I last covered WisdomTree, its shares were trading at a relatively undemanding valuation of about 12x forward earnings, being at the time a good buying opportunity for long-term investors in my opinion. Not surprisingly, its shares have performed quite well since then, up by more than 57% compared to just 15% for the overall stock market during the same period.

This good performance has been supported by a general recovery of growth stocks, but also by WisdomTree’s good operating performance in recent quarters and its efforts to grow its business through acquisitions.

Indeed, WisdomTree has recently announced an agreement to acquire Ceres Partners, LLC, a U.S.-based alternative asset manager that is specialized on farmland investments. This expands WisdomTree’s product offering into the private assets market, specifically on real estate and farmland. While this was an unexpected move by the company to some extent, it expands its assets under management (AuM) and gives it another growth source over the long term, being complementary to other asset classes already offered to customers.

Ceres Partners was founded in 2007 and has nowadays about $1.85 billion of AuM, managing some 545 U.S. farmland properties across 12 states, and has a positive track record and good growth prospects across farmland, and potentially new areas such as solar, AI data infrastructure, and water. Moreover, farmland has positive risk-adjusted returns historically and low correlation to traditional asset classes, being therefore an interesting diversification solution to WisdomTree’s customers.

WisdomTree is expected to pay about $275 million cash upfront when the transaction closes and up to $225 million earn-out consideration in 2030. This last payment is contingent on Ceres Partners achieving compounded annual revenue growth of 12-22%, measured over five years. The deal is expected to close by the last quarter of 2025, subject to regulatory approval.

According to WisdomTree, this deal is expected to be financially accretive as Ceres Partners margins are higher than compared to WisdomTree and the transaction (upfront payment) is expected to be fully financed through debt. While this acquisition only adds about $1.8 billion of AuM, or about 1.3% of WisdomTree’s AuMs, the impact on revenue is much higher. Indeed, Ceres Partners has a much higher revenue yield than compared to WisdomTree, adding about $39 million of annual revenues representing close to 9% of WisdomTree’s current annualized revenues. Regarding the valuation, WisdomTree is paying about 17x earnings, which seems cheap considering that WisdomTree is currently trading at a similar valuation, for a business that has much higher margins and good growth prospects ahead.

To finance this acquisition, the company has issued a convertible bond some days ago raising $415 million through a private offering, which is more than enough to finance its upfront payment expected to be paid until the end of this year. This offering was made to institutional investors at an annual cost of 4.625% and matures in 2030, and the total amount was subsequently raised by more $60 million after initial investors exercised their option to buy an additional amount. This means WisdomTree was able to raise $475 million from debt investors, or about $464 million of net proceeds from this issue after fees and expenses. Beyond funding the acquisition of Ceres Partners, the company also intends to use $37 million of the proceeds to buy back most of its outstanding convertible bond due 2028, about $90 million to repurchase its own shares, while the remaining should be used for general corporate purposes.

Regarding its most recent operating performance, WisdomTree has reported its first half results at the beginning of the month, reporting a positive operating momentum.

Its total AuM reached a new record high of $126 billion (+15% YoY), boosted by net inflows and positive market appreciation during the second quarter of the year. Its net inflows amounted to more than $3.5 billion in the last quarter, following a very strong Q1 with net inflows above $3 billion, showing that its strategy to diversify its product offering its bearing fruits.

This positive trend was maintained through July, with AuMs reaching more than $128 billion and net flows above $300 million, boding well for further revenue and earnings growth in the coming quarters. The inflows were broad based across the U.S. and Europe, while by types of products it was also diversified across international equity, U.S. equity and fixed income. Net inflows into digital assets products amounted to some $300 million in the first half of the year, being also a positive support for higher AuMs.

One important product to achieve strong net inflows was its new ETF of European Defense equities, which was launched back in March 2025 and has reached total AuM of $3.4 billion at the end of last June. This was clearly a successful new product, showing that WisdomTree continues to adapt its product offerings to what customers want, being an important support for sustainable business growth over the long term.

Given this background of higher AuMs, WisdomTree’s revenues increased to $112 million in the last quarter, up by 4.2% from the previous one. During the first half of the year, revenues were up by more than 8% compared to the same period of 2024, due to higher average AuM and other revenues. However, due to unfavorable foreign exchange movements, the company’s operating expenses have increased at a higher rate than expected and third-party distributions costs were also above its estimates, putting some pressure on WisdomTree’s profitability. This explains why despite the company being able to report good revenue growth, its adjusted net income was $25.9 million in Q2 2025, down by 4.4% YoY. For the rest of the year, the company has maintained its guidance more or less unchanged, apart from operating expenses that are expected to remain pressured by foreign exchange movements.

WisdomTree expects to maintain a positive growth path during the second half of 2025, supported by its strategy of expanding its product offering and by increasing its customer engagement, plus the integration of Ceres Partners will also be an important step to expand its product offering and enter into a new asset class with higher margins than compared to its existing ETF and digital assets business.

Another growth source that has shown very good results is its models offering, with assets under administration reaching $5.2 billion at the end of last June, already at its target for the full year 2025, and the total number of users being above 2,800 and well on track to achieve its target of about 3,250 by year-end. This offering is growing quite well showing that is a valuable tool for financial advisors, and should also lead to higher penetration of the company’s products among its customer base and lead to more recurring AuMs over the long term.

Regarding its balance sheet, the company maintained a positive profile at the end of Q2 2025, increasing its cash position to more than $290 million (vs. $267 million at the end of 2024) and leading to a lower leverage position as the company’s EBITDA increased during the first half of 2025. For the full year, its EBITDA is expected to be close to $170 million, while its net debt should be about $250 million. This means its net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is expected to be slightly below 1.5x, slightly lower than compared to its 1.54x ratio at the end of 2024, being an acceptable leverage position.

Given this background, it’s likely that WisdomTree will maintain unchanged its quarterly dividend of $0.03 per share ahead, which leads to a dividend yield of less than 1%. This shows that its management is not much concerned about providing an attractive income stream for shareholders, preferring instead to perform share buybacks to return more capital to shareholders.

Regarding its valuation, WisdomTree is currently trading at about 17x forward earnings, being a slight discount to its own historical average over the past five years. While this is much higher than compared to when I last analyzed WisdomTree (it was trading at about 12x earnings at the time), I think this is still an interesting valuation for a company that has good growth prospects and is making several initiatives to strengthen its business model.

Conclusion

WisdomTree has maintained a good operating performance in recent quarters, and its decision to enter into private assets seems to make sense giving it a new growth source over the long term. The expected acquisition of Ceres Partners seems to be highly complementary to its product offering and the price paid seems reasonable, making WisdomTree’s investment case even more compelling for long-term investors and, therefore, I maintain my Buy recommendation on its shares.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club: This Dip Is A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
August 22nd ended up being a rather painful day for club warehouse enterprise BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BJ). After management reported revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share that all fell short of analysts’ expectations for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, shares of the business dropped around 8% to. Even so, the firm has not been doing poorly over the long haul. Since I rated it a Buy in May of 2023, the stock is still up 51.2%. Even though that falls short now of the 56.6% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw over the same window of time, it is still a rather solid gain.

Because of worsening market conditions, I wouldn't be surprised to see more downside in the near term. However, shares of the business do look to be trading on the cheap. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all continue to climb. And when you wrap all of this together into one picture, I would say that the bullish outlook that I had previously still makes sense. At the end of the day, I believe that the company deserves to keep the Buy rating I had on it back then.

Still solid even though headwinds are an issue

For the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, BJ's Wholesale Club reported results that fell short of analysts’ expectations. For instance, revenue came in around $100 million lower than what was expected. But even with this being the case, year over year performance was quite strong. Revenue for the company grew from $5.21 billion in the second quarter of 2024 to $5.38 billion in the second quarter of this year.

Some investors who are bearish about the company might point out the fact that comparable club sales dropped by 0.3%. But this was only because of fuel. The fact of the matter is that fuel prices have declined as of late. If we ignore this, comparable sales grew by 2.3% year over year. On top of this, the company benefited from continued expansion. The number of club locations that it has in operation came in at 255. This was up from the 244 it had one year earlier. In addition to this, the number of BJ’s Gas locations that the company has grew from 178 to 190.

As the company expands its physical presence, it continues to benefit from a rising number of members. It ended the latest quarter with around 8 million members. That compares to the more than 7.5 million it reported having at the end of 2024. It is also worth noting that digital revenue for the company jumped 34% year over year.

Bottom line results also disappointed. Even though earnings per share grew from $1.08 to $1.14, this figure ultimately fell short of analysts’ expectations to the tune of $0.05 per share. Adjusted earnings per share increased from $1.09 to $1.14. That also ended up being $0.05 below what analysts had hoped to see. While this is disappointing, this still translates to a rise in net profits from $145 million to $150.7 million, and a rise in adjusted net profits from $146.3 million to $151.5 million. Other profitability metrics also grew year over year, with operating cash flow expanding from $221.4 million to $249.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $219.7 million to $253.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $281.3 million to $303.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all increased across the board and without exception. The business is clearly doing well as it continues to execute its growth strategy.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, the firm expects comparable club sales, excluding fuel revenue, to rise by between 2% and 3.5%. This guidance matches the guidance that management provided when it announced results for the first quarter of the year. Adjusted earnings per share guidance has also been increased from between $4.10 and $4.30 to between $4.20 and $4.35. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of $566.5 million compared to the $541.1 million the company reported for last year. If we assume that the first half of this year is indicative of what the rest of the year will look like on a year over year basis, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of about $971.5 million and EBITDA of $1.24 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the company for the last three completed fiscal years. The overall trend here has been positive, with the chart below illustrating that the rise in location count, combined with the rise in comparable sales, ended up pushing overall performance for the business higher. This is certainly a positive trend. And what makes things even better is that management is just getting started.

According to management, the U.S. warehouse club market is worth around $298 billion. Using results from last year, this means that BJ's Wholesale Club has a market share of only 6.9%. From 2007 to 2024, the even larger total retail space, excluding the automotive category, grew at an annualized rate of 6.2%. That ultimately took it up to $5.9 trillion in size.

The really great thing about the business model that BJ's Wholesale Club has championed is that the membership structure brings in additional revenue compared to if this was just your typical retail establishment. According to management, members generate a return of 10 times on their membership fee in the form of lower prices, access to the firm's quality brand products that it owns, lower priced fuel and services, attractive credit card or awards, and more. This gives the company a loyal base that it can also generate additional revenue from. And on top of this, it has enjoyed a meaningful rise in overall revenue associated with these membership fees.

Way back in 1997, the company generated only $56 million in revenue from the members that had shopping at its 84 clubs. But by last year, this had expanded to $457 million spread across the 250 clubs that it has in operation. This is high margin revenue that the business enjoys. And long term, management expects this model to continue to pay off. They anticipate comparable club sales growth to be at the low to mid-single digit rate for the foreseeable future. And from that, they anticipate total annualized revenue growth that is in the mid-single digit range. When you add on top of this operating efficiencies that the company should achieve because of economies of scale, it is forecasting long term earnings per share growth to be at the high single digit to low double digit rate.

Another positive thing about the business is that shares are actually quite affordable compared to what other similar companies go for. In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using historical results from 2024 and the aforementioned estimates for 2025. In the table below, I then compared BJ's Wholesale Club to three other similar firms. On both a price to earnings basis and on a price to operating cash flow basis, I found that it is the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, on an EV to EBITDA basis, only one of the companies was cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
BJ's Wholesale Club 22.6 13.2 10.7
Walmart (WMT) 41.6 20.9 19.8
PriceSmart (PSMT) 23.3 15.0 10.3
Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST) 54.5 34.4 34.0

To illustrate for you what kind of upside potential the company might warrant, we need only look at the chart below. In it, you can see how much upside or downside shares would experience if they were to trade at the same multiples as the lowest of the three competitors I compared it to, and at the average of those three competitors. In the worst case, we would be looking at downside of 3.8%. But when we start using the averages, we see upside of between 76.1% and 103.3%.

Takeaway

Right now, investors are a bit more pessimistic because of the revenue and earnings miss that the company experienced for the quarter. Even though management believes that the second half of this year is going to be strong enough to justify the upward revision in profitability that management has provided, market participants are clearly not sold on this view.

For those focused on the long haul, I would say that BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. stock looks relatively cheap and management has a solid track record of growth. Long term, I expect that growth to continue. Because of this, I do believe that maintaining it as a Buy candidate makes sense.

data.title: Fuchs SE: Guidance Cut Provides Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Recent key developments

Following my long thesis on Fuchs SE (OTCPK:FUPEF) (OTCPK:FUPBY) in June 2025, there have been three major developments:

1) The German government proposed growth incentives which should add a couple of percentage points to net profit margins at Fuchs as corporate taxes are likely to be lowered from 2028. As a result, the current overall tax burden on companies should decrease from nearly 30% at present to about 25% in 2032.

This development has been largely ignored by the stock market.

2) Fuchs lowered its 2025 guidance in July:

"For the financial year 2025, FUCHS now expects sales and EBIT on previous year’s level (financial year 2024: Sales at €3,525 million, EBIT at €434 million). The previous outlook for 2025 expected sales at around €3.7 billion and EBIT at around €460 million. Consensus for the financial year 2025 stands at €3,660 million for sales and at €459 million for EBIT.

The adjustment of the outlook results from a continued muted demand of relevant customer groups due to tariff discussions triggered by the US, slow industrial production in Europe as well as from ongoing geopolitical tensions. FUCHS currently expects the overall weak economic environment to continue in the second half of 2025."

3) Fuchs released its full Q2/25 results two weeks later.

Both stocks suffered from the disappointing near-term outlook, although a bottom was quickly found, as investors had already anticipated a sluggish development on the backdrop of the current macro environment. In addition, the Q2/25 earnings call provided a positive long-term outlook.

Q2/25 earnings call insights

The weaker USD clearly burdened results in EUR terms, while the previously strong U.S. business did not grow at all. Moreover, the new large-volume (albeit lower-margin) business taken over in the U.S. required start-up investments.

"Most of the stuff is toll manufactured for us in the U.S. But for us, it's a part of our strategy because on that business in other world markets, we earn very good money in Asia and in Europe. And therefore ... we want to take on the business, learn with that business and then multiply it positively in the future as we did in other markets."

The EBIT shortfall of the North and South America business segment is more than the entire group's shortfall. Hence, if this relatively small segment (19% of total sales) had not struggled with forex, one-off M&A costs, start-up costs for the new high-volume business and the trade war issues, Fuchs would have delivered the expected growth in H1/25.

As far as tariffs are concerned, there is, however, a silver lining: As mentioned in my recent thesis, after the recent investment cycle, Fuchs can now manufacture its full portfolio locally both in the U.S. and in China – in contrast to many of its competitors which are still importing from abroad in China and in the U.S. This situation could obviously create some interesting pricing and/or market share opportunities.

While the analyst community is very much focused on near-term margin developments, according to management, margins are not considered a problem – it is sales that have come in worse than expected due to the trade war causing massive order delays and overall hesitation.

"When you talk about top line and margin because if you look at the margin, 35.1% on the gross profit, we are right in our target area. Margin is not a problem. The problem is that the top line is not there where we have anticipated it to be and all our expenses are inflated, especially on the personnel side and on the freight side. And that's one part where we have a serious look at the moment to make sure we have a better conversion from additional sales and margins into the profit part."

Already initiated cost-cutting initiatives should partially compensate for the sluggish sales development in the second half.

Bottom line

Although there may be little to be happy about in the near term, my long-term thesis is perfectly intact: Fuchs is highly likely to gain market share, and the start-up investments in the U.S. will level off. Once there is certainty about tariffs, and businesses rebuild their inventories, sales should grow strongly. At that point in time, the upscaled organization will actually be needed and not pressure profit margins anymore.

Trading for just under 14x this year's reduced EPS estimates (the ordinary shares) and with strong and quite predictable growth prospects ahead, this quality compounder is far too cheap.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The AI Bubble Is Leaking: Prepare For A Major Market Reversal
data.description:
Ever since ChatGPT took the world by storm and NVIDIA (NVDA) delivered blowout guidance in the early months of 2023, the AI trade has dominated the markets. Whether it be leading AI players like NVDA and Palantir (PLTR) or some of the traditional megacap tech powerhouses like Meta (META), AI-related stocks have far and away crushed the broader markets over that period of time.

While AI is truly an exciting and revolutionary technology that I expect to deliver incredible value to the global economy in the coming decades, cautions being raised by the likes of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman—that AI is in a bubble and that investors are getting overly excited—should give investors pause about what price they are paying for exposure to future earnings power from AI.

Between these indicators and very frothy market valuations, I believe that the AI bubble is starting to leak and that prudent investors should position their portfolios for a major market reversal. In this article, we will detail why and share how we are positioning our portfolio accordingly.

Growing AI And Market Concerns

While analysts have been growing increasingly bullish on the company, raising their growth estimates even as its stock price has pulled back meaningfully recently, PLTR is perhaps the poster child for why AI is in a major bubble. It currently trades at a whopping 210 times forward earnings and 518 times trailing earnings. While this may not seem like an outrageously high valuation given that analysts are expecting it to deliver 35.7% CAGR in earnings per share through 2029, even if you assume that it achieves that growth rate at its current stock price alone, its 2029 P/E ratio would still be an incredible 73x.

This means that PLTR is going to have to sustain a very high earnings per share growth rate for many years into the future to justify its current valuation and deliver a reasonable rate of return to shareholders buying at today's price. As a result, I think that it could easily lose another 50% or even more of its value, based on its current very rosy growth estimates from analysts, and still be considered richly priced.

On top of that, MIT recently released a study showing that a whopping 95% of companies pursuing generative AI initiatives are seeing little to no financial returns on investment. While there are many reasons for this—including challenges with integrating generative AI into existing workflows, human error, and other inefficiencies—it still raises serious questions about AI’s ability to deliver significant value for companies in the near term.

As such, this study further accentuates concerns that AI may be in a bubble, with investors getting overly excited about AI’s capability to create real-world value on a large scale in the near future. It also raises major doubts about Palantir’s valuation, as just because companies are taking them on and the U.S. government is giving them increased revenue does not necessarily mean that they will be able to deliver commensurate value in exchange for that money. This could challenge the durability of those contracts and, in particular, the durability of their growth runway.

Even if they are delivering value for that money, if the broader perception shifts such that AI is not as value-add as previously thought, companies may become more hesitant to pay the large sums required to bring on Palantir and similar firms to try to enhance their operations. Additionally, if AI is not as value-add as previously thought, it likely means that major technical breakthroughs are still needed before it can deliver on its promise.

These include substantial improvements in agentic AI, which could eventually displace significant white-collar jobs once LLMs are able to operate autonomously on computers to perform a wide range of tasks that currently require office employees. Another major breakthrough that will likely be required is significant advances in autonomous robotics, which would enable AI to perform real-world tasks ranging from assembly line work to restaurant operations, logistics warehouses, and even agricultural labor. Robots could also eventually take on many tasks currently performed in the construction industry and some of the trades, which in turn would devastate large swaths of blue-collar work. However, the technologies needed to truly displace humans in both of these domains still seem quite far away.

As a result, the question now becomes: how long are investors willing to wait to see true bottom-line profits from AI? Will they be willing to wait patiently while these companies burn tremendous amounts of cash engaging in R&D and infrastructure build-out, or will they begin to grow impatient and demand profits to begin hitting the bottom line? If the latter becomes the reality, then we could see a major pullback in the valuations of AI stocks ranging from PLTR to the leading megacap tech stocks.

How I Am Investing Right Now

With those concerns in mind, what does this mean for how we are positioning our portfolio? When you consider the bubble-like conditions and the risks for a short-term pullback in the AI space, and then extrapolate to the broader S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) where leading valuation models also seem to indicate that the market is highly overvalued, and couple that with numerous geopolitical and macroeconomic risks—such as the growing risk of conflict in the Far East, ongoing tensions and proxy wars between Israel and Iran in the Middle East, uncertainties regarding the outcome of the Russia-Ukraine war, continued tariff risks, runaway deficits in the United States and elsewhere, and the ever-present risk of inflation re-accelerating and what impact that could have on long-term interest rates in the U.S., especially when paired with persistent deficit spending—there are many reasons to be concerned about investing in broader equity markets today.

In fact, all it would take is an AI bubble bursting, and the S&P 500 would likely plunge, given that tech stocks make up about a third of its underlying portfolio. As a result, we are steering clear of broader index funds as well as megacap tech stocks. Instead, we are looking at areas of the market where there are strong fundamentals, recession resistance, and very attractive valuations. We think these investments should do well regardless of what happens in the macroeconomic setting or how the AI saga plays out.

In particular, we like REITs (VNQ) and infrastructure (UTF) right now, with blue-chip opportunities like Realty Income (O), Rayonier (RYN), Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), and Oneok (OKE), all offering highly compelling dividend yields and valuations backed by strong balance sheets and durable and defensive business models.

Investor Takeaway

The AI boom has generated tremendous wealth over the past several years, and we have admittedly missed out on much of it due to our focus on income-generating securities. We have nonetheless managed to outperform the market through our opportunistic capital recycling program. We believe that our portfolio is positioned for particularly strong performance moving forward, given our focus on AI-agnostic, blue-chip, deeply undervalued stocks, and it should benefit from a major market rotation out of AI into more profitable names as investors grow more concerned about the profitability of AI in the near term.

On top of that, with bubble-like market valuations and numerous macro downside catalysts brewing, I think that right now is a prudent time to take a more defensive posture in one’s portfolio. This is the approach we are taking at High Yield Investor.

data.title: MPLX: Growing Almost 8% Distribution In A Strong Package
data.description:
MPLX LP (NYSE: NYSE:MPLX) is one of the largest midstream companies, with a strong more than 7.5% dividend yield and a remarkably strong balance sheet. The company has outperformed a tech-heavy market YTD since we last recommended investing. Despite that, as we'll see throughout this article the company has room to continue its growth and shareholder returns.

Note: This article discusses an MLP, which has substantial tax differences from a standard corporation, which might affect the taxes you pay. For investors unfamiliar with the segment we recommend starting here: MLP 101: The Basics - Energy Infrastructure Council

MPLX Business Update

The company had $1.7 billion in adjusted EBITDA in the quarter, a mid single-digit YoY increase.

At the same time, the company has announced an acquisition of Northwest Midstream, which we'll discuss in more detail below with 200K dedicated acres to expand what's already a strong midstream position. The company has continued a very strong dividend policy of more than 7.5% and rare in the MLP world it's repurchased $100 million of shares.

At its dividend yield, we'd like to see it ramp this up, but overall, the company's commitment to shareholder returns is well positioned.

MPLX Northwest Midstream

The company announced an almost $2.4 billion acquisition of Northwest Midstream at a 7x forecast EBITDA.

We're not a big fan of the acquisition being based on EBITDA 2 years from now but since Northwest Midstream is in the middle of a big capital expansion it's not particularly surprising. The company is almost tripling its treatment capacity of NG while almost doubling acid gas injection. The asset is also well positioned to be integrated into MLPX's impressive processing system.

The acquisition adds 200+ miles of gathering pipelines from more than 200K dedicated acres with strong fee-based cash flow. As seen with the map of MPLX's assets and processing plants it integrates well into MPLX's existing assets which enables strong operational synergies and MPLX to become the preferred provider to get assets downstream to the market.

MPLX Permian-Focused Growth

Overall, MPLX has a strong Permian Basin asset portfolio, with 10% of U.S. natural gas flowing through the company's portfolio.

The company has impressive treatment and processing capacity along with expanding long haul capacity. Downstream that goes to the company's fractionation capacity and eventual export terminal JV. The company is building incremental capacity at each stage here, a lot of which will come in the next 1-2 years from the company's capital programs.

The company is deploying over $5 billion for growth, and we expect this to be largely accretive for the company's goals.

MPLX 2025

The company has a substantial $5 billion planned growth portfolio that will come in service over the next few years.

The company has a number of assets and acquisitions going in service in 2025 including a 25% increase in the Matterhorn pipeline and doubling the BANGL pipeline. The vast majority of the company's growth plans are in natural gas & NGLs, much longer lived assets than oil, and experiencing substantial long term demand from power plants and LNG.

Those downstream assets can provide decades of reliability providing strong midstream position.

MPLX Shareholder Returns

The company remains committed to driving substantial shareholder returns off of this asset portfolio, with growing assets and the recent acquisition.

The company has managed to grow both adjusted EBITDA and DCF with modest growth although we expect growth to accelerate. The company's almost 8% distribution yield is comfortably covered with a 1.5x coverage ratio even if the coverage ratio has gone down due to distribution growth.

The company has a consolidated 3.1x debt to LTM EBITDA ratio, a more than manageable debt yield which enables it to put cash towards distributions and growth rather than debt and balance sheet improvement.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the company's asset build around natural gas and NGLs which are seeing massive investment from a number of providers due to LNG and datacenter expansion. A market recession that decreases AI investment could substantially decline natural gas demand and result in an overbuild of assets hurting the company's future returns.

Conclusion

MPLX has a strong and distributed portfolio of assets. This includes key assets like the Matterhorn Express Pipeline and other Permian Basin assets that integrate well into the company's portfolio. The company's recent Northwest Midstream acquisition adds assets that integrate well into the company's existing portfolio.

The company has raised its distribution substantially, pushing down its coverage rate. However, at a 1.5x coverage ratio, the company still has substantial room to continue its distribution of more than 7.5%. The company at the same time is investing heavily in growth assets for the next few years, a combination that will support continued returns.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Shale Producers Flock Back To Hedging
data.description:
By Amanda Townsley

At a Glance

  • U.S. shale producers, notably quiet on hedging since the pandemic, are re-engaging in WTI futures and options
  • The emergence of this key seller reflects the shift in the long term oil price outlook and the ability of U.S. producers to proactively manage revenue through an uncertain time

A significant shift is underway in oil markets: U.S. shale producers – whose willingness to hedge their oil production had been notably muted since the pandemic – are re-engaging in earnest, marking the return of what once was a major seller on the oil futures curve. The re-emergence of a key player is drawing keen attention from traders, who carefully watch participant behavior patterns to guide their strategy.

Data compiled by Bloomberg Intelligence shows that hedging activity by publicly-owned oil and gas companies began to tick higher in the second half of 2024 and has continued throughout 2025. Weekly reports from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) on futures and options positioning confirm the trend continued into the third quarter, with Swap Dealer Short positions* in WTI futures and options still rising.

What’s Changed?

Briefly in 2024 and again in 2025, the price of WTI futures dipped into the fifties, levels not seen since 2021, pushing drilling economics into uncharted territory for the new, restructured shale industry. Since 2020, consolidation, ample revenues and a focus on returning capital to shareholders has changed the balance sheets and thus the hedging requirements and hedging appetite of the shale industry. A bullish outlook for oil and steep backwardation in the oil futures curve further discouraged producers from locking in prices that seemed too low.

Conditions have changed. While acute geopolitical flashpoints can inject short-term upside into oil prices, the consensus among leading energy agencies (the IEA, EIA and OPEC) points toward a trajectory of ample, if not oversupplied, conditions in the medium to long term. The oil price has been reflective of this dichotomy as front month WTI futures, a main indicator of revenue for producers, swung from a high of $80 per barrel in January 2025 to lows of $55 in the spring before rallying back to $77 in June.

Rallies have given producers an opportunity to lock in strong returns, while threats of negative drilling economics and a less-backwardated curve have also encouraged hedging at more modest price levels. No matter the level, hedging allows producers to strategically position themselves for both episodic shocks and a more tempered long-term price environment.

Producer hedging peaked in 2018, both in absolute terms and as a percentage of U.S. production. Between 2019 and 2023, hedging levels experienced a gradual decline as older hedges matured, producers unwound positions during the pandemic-induced price decline and overall hedging appetite diminished. The renewed appetite aligns with a broader market sentiment anticipating a moderation in price growth.

The Hedger’s Tool Kit

Producers who want to lock in a fixed price for crude oil often sell a WTI swap, allowing them to exchange a fixed payment for the calendar month average (CMA) of the front-month WTI futures price, either in the OTC market or via the Exchange’s WTI CMA future (CS). The popularity of this hedge is tied to its alignment with physical trade: physical crude sales are frequently settled as a differential to this NYMEX WTI CMA. In July 2025, open interest (OI) on this contract hit a post-pandemic high. Bloomberg data also reflects producers managing their Midland and Gulf Coast basis risk using a rising volume of Argus WTI Houston and Argus WTI Midland-based derivatives.

In the pre-pandemic period of active shale hedging, complex option structures were prominent, reflecting producers’ sophisticated understanding of options markets and the ability to tailor custom risk profiles. Three-way collars – which combine a long put, a short call and a second, further out-of-the-money short put – offered producers cost-efficient downside protection while strategically defining their upside and re-exposure levels. Two-way collars, consisting of a long put and a short call, were also common.

In 2024 and earlier in 2025, public company hedging focused more heavily on buying outright puts, signaling a direct desire for pure downside protection against price declines. This move proved to be positive after oil prices declined sharply in the spring.

WTI options volume is up 40% year-on-year in the first half of 2025, with the producer-oriented average-price option volumes reflecting significant activity both on price rallies and near the lows. The significant increase in OI in WTI CMA futures (CS) and the average price WTI option (AO), directly reflects this renewed and purposeful hedging activity.

The landscape of oil price hedging for U.S. shale producers has undeniably undergone significant shifts since 2020. While the trajectory for U.S. crude oil production growth is uncertain, the increased willingness of producers to sell forward and secure prices signifies the industry’s ability to manage revenue and lay a stable foundation for the coming years. With hedged positions now growing and producers showing a clear appetite to engage with the oil futures and options market, they are once again a key participant for oil traders to watch.

*Swap Dealer short positions widely regarded as a proxy for the futures positions taken by banks to offset the over-the-counter (OTC) hedges they execute with producers.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Automatic Data Processing: Stability You Can Count On, But Valuation Keeps Us Neutral
data.description:
Introduction

Automatic Data Processing, Inc. (NASDAQ:ADP) is a leading provider of business outsourcing services such as payroll management, human resource management and other administrative tasks. Founded in 1979, the company now serves over 1 million clients in more than 140 countries, processing payroll for an estimated 42 million workers. The company’s business model runs on high switching costs, trusted brand reputation and extensive industry experience. ADP also enjoys extremely high client retention due to this with most of its clients averaging a tenure of 10+ years with the company

Investment Grade

I give ADP a HOLD rating. The company’s resilient business model, strong client retention, and 50+ years of consistent dividend growth make it a defensive compounder well-suited for long-term, income-focused investors. However, at the current market price above $300, ADP trades at a significant premium to its estimated intrinsic value of ~$241 per share.

Unique Selling Points

Extensive Global Scale and Reach: ADP serves clients ranging from the biggest multinational corporations (MNCs) to small businesses as well, making sure they remain omnipresent across all market segments. With over a million clients worldwide, the company operates on a massive scale which cannot be matched by their competitors or any new entrants. This makes it uniquely capable of handling cross country payrolls and HR needs for enterprises of all sizes. The company provides services such as RUN powered by ADP, making payroll and HR management seamless and easy to use for small enterprises as well as GlobalView to meet the requirements of large enterprises.

Access to Proprietary Data and Compliance: With a client base of over a million, ADP has accumulated the largest proprietary dataset on workforce and payroll information over the years, helping in boost innovation within its services. ADP has direct access to on-the-ground data on hiring, payrolls, and HR metrics through which the company works continuously to innovate its services, each new client's data making adding value to ADP’s insights. Operating with sensitive payroll data and regulations give the company another competitive edge in terms of compliance management. The company has successfully used its experience to turn regulatory complexity to its own advantage such as automated tax filing and compliance alerts. This “moat of compliance” gives the company an upper hand as now companies look to find solutions for ethical use of AI in workspaces, ADP with its already extensive experience, is a go-to for any business.

Dividend Analysis

Strong Dividend Growth: The company has successfully been able to pay out dividends for 50 consecutive years, placing the company among the Dividend Aristocrats and Dividend Kings. This track record reflects the stability of the company’s business model and its commitment to deliver cash to its shareholders even during economic downturns. ADP has kept raising its dividend during the 2008 global financial crisis as well as during the 2020 COVID pandemic. ADP’s dividends have been so strong it has often been used as a benchmark stock for income oriented investors and for those who compare dividends.

Dividend Yield and Growth Rate: As of 2025 the company has maintained a dividend yield of 2%, while this remains moderate when compared to the S&P 500 average, ADP compensates for this with a strong dividend growth rate. The company has maintained an annual compounded growth rate for its dividend at 11.6%, while the company’s annualized dividend was $6.16 per share in 2025, up from roughly $3.55 five years prior.

Most recently, the company increased its quarterly dividend to $1.54 a share, reflecting management’s confidence in its cash flows, investors may go on to assume a high single digit growth or a lower double digit growth in dividend if the company continues to follow projected trends.

Payout Ratio and Sustainability: ADP’s dividends are supported by strong earnings and cash projection, indicating a high degree of sustainability. The company targets a payout ratio in the 55%-60% range of annual earnings. This level remains prudent, meaning almost 45%-40% are retained to reinvest in company growth or share buybacks.

With low amounts of debt and consistent profits there is a low risk of a dividend cut barring an unprecedented collapse in the business. In fact, ADP has proved time and time again that its business remains recession-proof, proving this by increasing dividend in 2008-2009 and 2020-2021.

Business Risks

Data Security and Privacy Threats: ADP remains a primary target for cyber attack thanks to its control over sensitive information over personal and financial data. A major security breach in the company would disrupt services, lead to theft of confidential data, face legal and regulatory penalties, and damage its reputation and trustworthiness. This could lead to difficulty in maintaining the current set of clients and onboarding new ones as well. The ever-changing landscape of privacy laws and extra pressure on the ethical and transparent use of AI adds compliance risk exerting extra pressure on the company to continually adapt its practices.

Regulatory and Macroeconomic Factors: ADP, being a service provider, may be significantly influenced by changes in regulations and economic conditions. On the Regulatory side, laws governing payroll taxes, labor, benefits, and co-employment can affect ADP’s operations. For example, if tax authorities change rules to allow less time for companies to remit withheld taxes, ADP’s clients would hold funds for shorter durations, directly reducing the average client fund balances that ADP can invest and earn interest on. On the macroeconomic front, market downturns or labour market contractions may pressure the company’s growth. It is often seen in a weaker economy situation, businesses tend to lay their employees off resulting in lower transactional volumes for the company. A severe downturn may even lead the company to lose smaller clients mainly as they shut down and cancel services. The company mitigates this risk by maintaining a strong clientele of all sizes in multiple industries.

Financial Risk

Client Fund Interest: ADP collects client payroll, taxes, and wages in advance and temporarily holds these funds before remittance. ADP invests these funds into safe securities such as corporate bonds, asset backed securities and money market securities earning interest income on these investments and thus contributing to their profits. This opens the company up to interest rate fluctuation risk: when rates are rising then ADP earns more on its funds as seen in Q2 2025 where the company earned $273 million — a 21% jump. In the opposite scenario, if interest rates were to drop, the revenue earned on this investment would decrease as well. Regulatory changes as stated earlier may add up to this as if a law were to be passed for faster tax remittance, it would reduce the time the company held its investment hence dragging its earnings down.

Credit and Liquidity Factors: ADP maintains a conservative balance sheet, with total assets of $54.4 billion vs. $49.8 billion in liabilities, with a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.66x. Net debt remained at $792 million reflecting a low leverage profile however, continued access to low cost financing is still required for certain operations and thus maintaining a strong credit rating becomes key. If the company’s credit rating were downgraded or the credit markets tighten their borrowing costs would rise leading to reduction of margins or increased interest expense. ADP’s investment portfolio must also be handled sensibly to avoid losses, even though the company does invest in safe securities, rapid interest rate moves may affect the market.

Earning Analysis

Consistent Growth Trajectory: In 2024 the company reported a revenue growth of 6.6% to $19.2 billion and net income increased by 9.96% to $3.75 billion. ADP's growth has been consistent YoY leading to the management forecasting a 6%-7% revenue growth and 7%-9% EPS growth for this financial year (FY 2025). In line with history, ADP has maintained a reputation of beating management guidance and analyst estimates, every quarter of FY 2024. Analysts have predicted the company to rise from $20.5 billion in 2025 to ~$22.8 billion by 2027, a CAGR of 6% and increase in net income from $4 billion to $4.9 billion in the same period. The company’s earnings are stable with moderate growth, investors may also remain relaxed incase of a recession as ADP famously increased its EPS during 2008-2009.

Strong Profitability and Cash Flows: ADP has generated robust profit margins and returns, indicative of its efficiency and competitive moat. The company has been expanding its operating margin gradually as of the latest quarter it stood around 25% with net profit margin in FY 2024 standing at 19.5%. ADP’s focus on cost discipline and scalable cloud platforms has helped widen its margins. ADP’s capital return metrics have been outstanding with an ROE of 76%, well above the industry average of 23-25%. In FY2024, the company produced $4.16 billion in operating cash flow and about $3.59 billion in free cash flow after capital expenditures. ADP being a service company does not require high capital expenditure, this means the company has the ability to turn most of its profits back as cash, be returned to shareholders or be reinvested to generate further profits

Competitor Analysis

ADP vs Paychex

Market Position and Scale: Both ADP and Paychex, Inc. (PAYX) are leaders in payroll processing, but ADP is significantly larger and more globally diversified. ADP’s market capitalization of $119 billion is more than double that of Paychex’s $50 billion. In terms of revenue, ADP generated roughly $19 billion in FY 2024 compared to about $5.6 billion for Paychex. ADP serves over 1 million clients worldwide, including many large enterprises with employees across multiple countries compared to Paychex, serving around 800,000 clients primarily in the United States and a few other markets focusing on small to medium-sized businesses.

Service Offerings: ADP provides a customizable, scalable ecosystem, ranging from small payroll processing to building comprehensive HCM systems for large enterprises. Paychex focuses on simplicity and cost effective packages, best for organizations with 50-100 employees. Paychex’s flagship platform, Flex is designed to be an all in one payroll and HR tool capable of doing everything. It is easy to use for businesses without any dedicated HR staff and provides a simple payroll system for any type of worker along with 24/7 support. ADP’s strength remains in comprehensive and scalable solutions witch global capabilities while Paychex grows with streamlined and budget friendly services for domestic small businesses.

Financial Performance and Investor Perspective: Both companies are highly performing dividend paying companies. ADP has seen faster growth than that compared to Paychex, in September 2024 the company’s EPS grew 12.5% YoY compared to Paychex Q2 2024 of 6%. The same goes for revenue as well with ADP maintaining a growth of 6%-7% compared to Paychex’s 5%, mainly due to focus on smaller clients and a more mature core market. Both firms maintain quite strong margins, ADP’s operating margins are 25%, and Paychex’s operating margin is even higher around 36–37% in recent years due to its pure-play focus and less R&D spending. Paychex’s gross margin 70% exceeds ADP’s, but this can be attributed to different accounting for certain costs (e.g., insurance in PEO). ADP’s larger size and global spread arguably make it slightly less risky; its revenue is more diversified and it proved resilient in 2020’s global downturn (remaining profitable and dividend-increasing). Paychex, with its focus on U.S. small businesses, can be more exposed to domestic economic swings or competition in the SMB segment, but it has also shown resilience and rapid rebound after downturns

Valuation

The model starts from an assumed FCFF of $4.78 billion in Year 1, rising to $6.30 billion in Year 5. Revenues are predicted to increase from $21.6 billion to $28.0 billion during the forecast horizon, with operating margins improving from 25.9% to 27.5% as a result of ADP's ongoing drive for automation and scale benefits. I used a 7.10% discount rate, capturing ADP's low-risk profile, profitability, and low leverage. The capital structure is based on a largely equity-funded base, in keeping with the company's robust balance sheet. In the terminal year, I utilized a 2.5% long-term growth rate, a combination of inflation expectations and modest economic growth. Under these assumptions, I estimated an intrinsic value of $241.55 per share. At a market price of more than $300, ADP seems to be trading at a premium to its intrinsic value.

Who is This Stock Meant For?

ADP is suited for long term investors who are interested in dividend growth, stable earnings and a company with a reputable brand image. With more than 50 years of consecutive dividend growth and industry leadership in payroll and HR services, it provides a stable exposure to the growing digitalization of the global workforce. Its capital-light business with a strong FCF and conservative dividend payout policy help deliver sustainable shareholder returns. This is not a recommendation for growth oriented investors, but is rather tilted more towards those who desire a stable compounder with defensive qualities, improved client retention, and excellent competitive advantages. ADP fits perfectly for those stability investors seeking growth and along with durability.

Conclusion

ADP has been helping both big and small firms with its payroll and HR services for over several decades. Its size and customer relationships are hard to replicate. The company keeps innovating with investments in cloud based technology and AI while expanding its topline and yet delivering consistent dividends for over 50 years. Yes, competition does pose potential challenges, but the company’s rich history and prudent management keep it ahead. ADP is a solid, low stress investment for long term shareholders who desire stable growth and certain income. But just to consider a neutral stance based on the results from my valuation. I would rate ADP as a Hold.

data.title: JEPI: 2 Reasons To Buy It Right Now
data.description:
The JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (NYSEARCA:JEPI) has been growing rapidly in popularity thanks in large part to its attractive monthly distributions that it throws off.

The combination of a relatively low expense ratio, broad underlying diversification to leading blue-chip stocks, high yield, and monthly payouts has a lot of appeal to income-focused investors, turning JEPI into a fund with more than $41 billion in AUM.

That said, it has underperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) year to date by a factor of roughly 2 to 1. Looking at the chart below, you can learn a lot about how JEPI performs and when investors should buy it and when they should not.

In this article, I will look into this further and detail the two reasons why some investors may want to buy JEPI right now.

Why Investors May Want To Buy JEPI Right Now

As the chart above illustrates, JEPI significantly outperformed SPY during the early-year market sell-off in response to concerns about the negative impact of President Donald Trump’s tariff policies. This makes sense because JEPI is largely an S&P 500 proxy that overlays a covered call strategy on top of it, which helps it generate its significant monthly distributions.

In addition to making it a major income generator, what this strategy effectively accomplishes is creating a more level sequence of returns for the fund. The calls generate income that turns into alpha during down markets, but in strong bull markets, it leads to weaker total return performance since the upside for the fund is significantly capped by the covered calls it has sold. This dynamic has shown itself since April as the market has recovered sharply amid declining concerns about the potential for harmful economic impact from tariffs, leading to a strong rally in SPY while JEPI’s recovery has been much more muted.

This brings me to the two reasons to consider buying it right now. The first is that if you want an S&P 500 proxy for your income-focused portfolio but need to generate income from it to still meet your income needs, JEPI is a pretty good solution as it charges a 0.35% expense ratio, which is quite reasonable. It also combines a high single-digit to low double-digit annualized yield with monthly distributions that are ideal for someone looking to fund living expenses with income from investments while still giving very nice diversified underlying exposure that somewhat mirrors the S&P 500’s portfolio. This is especially true given that it has exposure to some leading mega-cap tech stocks that are often missing from income-focused portfolios.

The second big reason to own it is if you're looking for an S&P 500-like position that's somewhat hedged against near-term downside risk. As JEPI illustrated earlier this year, it's far from a perfect hedge, but for someone who still wants to generate significant income and preserve some upside potential, JEPI also offers a somewhat cushioned downside risk due to its covered call strategy. Meanwhile, the way it implements its covered call strategy is such that it still preserves some upside potential during rising markets.

Given that the majority of leading market valuation models—such as the Buffett Indicator, the price-to-earnings model, the price-to-sales model, the interest rate model, and the mean reversion model—all indicate that the market is significantly overvalued right now, and there's considerable concern that the AI boom may be on the verge of ending, with the likes of Sam Altman recently calling it a bubble and AI darling Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) stock pulling back sharply recently, now seems like a prudent time to take a more cautious approach to major market indexes like SPY.

Investor Takeaway

While JEPI has underperformed SPY—and I think over the long term it probably will continue to do so, given that the covered call approach tends to leave it materially behind during strong market rallies—there's a potential place for it as a small-sized position in an income-focused portfolio that's looking for diversification into larger stocks - especially in the mega-cap tech sector - that are often missing from income portfolios. It also could be useful as a tactical pick over shorter periods of time when investors want to take a more guarded yet still long approach to the S&P 500 when market conditions appear to be getting a bit too frothy.

While we can see the merits of JEPI for some investors in unique situations, we continue to prefer picking individual high-yield stocks and steering clear of covered call strategies in order to maximize upside and long-term total return potential in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Meta Platforms Stock Declines As AI Restructuring And Insider Sales Pressure Outlook (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) closed at $739.10 on August 21, marking a 1.15% drop for the session, with trading volume of $6.56 billion, placing it seventh in overall market activity. The move extends the stock’s steepest weekly decline since April, a 6% pullback, even as shares remain more than 25% higher year-to-date.

The immediate trigger for selling was the restructuring of Meta’s AI division, which included a hiring freeze and a reorganization into four teams, a step aimed at containing escalating labor costs in a competitive talent market.

Restructuring signals caution while growth stays intact

The changes underscore management’s attempt to strike a balance between efficiency and long-term innovation. While the decision signals cost control rather than retreat, it has unsettled investors already wary of stretched valuations. Optimists point to Meta’s durable advertising business and infrastructure buildout, including new data centers, while skeptics highlight insider selling, regulatory scrutiny, and a downgrade from Zacks Research that shifted its rating from strong buy to hold.

Meta stock price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

From a technical view, Meta’s decline pulled shares below the 20-day exponential moving average near $753, often a signal of short-term weakness. The stock now tests support around the 725–753 band, with the 50-day EMA at $725 a key pivot. A breakdown risks a move toward $690 where the 100-day EMA sits, while a rebound above $753 would restore momentum and open a path toward $780–$800.

Investor sentiment split between risks and opportunity

The RSI has slipped to 47 from above 60 earlier this month, reflecting cooling momentum. A drop below 45 could tip the balance toward further downside, while a rebound above 55 would suggest renewed buying strength. Meanwhile, insider sales by senior executives have weighed on confidence, and regulatory pressure over AI practices remains a looming cost risk. Yet Meta continues to expand its strategic footprint, including approval for a new Louisiana data center and a $100 million solar power deal, reinforcing its dual focus on technology scale and sustainability.

Broker views remain divided. BofA maintains a buy rating, citing early-stage AI opportunities, while Morgan Stanley has emphasized risks tied to stock-based compensation and margin pressure. This divergence reflects the core tension: Meta’s innovation capacity remains strong, but governance issues and expense growth temper near-term enthusiasm. For investors, the 725–753 zone will likely decide direction in the short run. A decisive move above resistance could restore confidence, while failure to hold support risks deepening the pullback. Elevated volume during the latest selloff suggests volatility could persist until one of these levels breaks.

In earlier analysis, we noted Meta’s advance through April and May was driven by heavy institutional positioning and confidence in its AI-led growth path. The latest retracement, while sharper than prior pullbacks, still fits within the channel structure that has guided its longer-term climb. Maintaining support will be key to confirming that the broader bullish framework remains intact.
